coverage_notes: Three independent plan-mode turns with long, multi-constraint prompts intended to stress the planner (rich unreal_ai.plan_dag: many nodes, realistic dependencies) and the executor (serial readiness across a–z style graphs). Not a golden-file suite—use headed or local runs to inspect DAG shape, wall time, and run_finished.success. Expect longer runs than plan-mode-smoke; each turn is a full plan pipeline (planner + all nodes).

turns.request: We are prepping a new gameplay test map for gray-box iteration in UE5.7. I want a structured plan, not a Wall of Text answer. Assume we might have third-person pawn, blockout meshes, a few placeholder lights, and that we care about play-in-editor sanity and collisions. Walk me through what I should verify and in what order: world partition vs single-level assumptions, default pawn / GameMode, player start placement, collision on blockout, lighting build vs dynamic preview, basic performance flags (Nanite vs traditional on blockout), and saving source control–friendly conventions. For anything that must happen before something else (for example, you cannot sensibly tune lighting until the map bounds and representative meshes exist), call that out in the ordering. The output should still be a proper plan DAG when you are in plan mode.

I need to harden a small feature slice that spans Blueprint and project settings: Enhanced Input mappings, a player-facing action that should feel responsive in PIE, and packaging sanity so designers do not ship with editor-only assets or broken input on a stripped build. Lay out a careful checklist that separates editor-time validation (does the Enhanced Input mapping asset exist, are dead mappings cleaned up, are contexts applied on the right pawn) from runtime validation (PIE, standalone game if relevant), and ends with packaging or staging checks (cook warnings, maps to include, default game map). Where steps depend on prior steps—like confirming the pawn class before validating its input component—encode that as a dependency-style flow in your plan rather than a flat bullet list only.

Picture a content-heavy milestone: new environment kit approved by art, but tech art has not yet signed off on LODs, materials using virtual texture usage, landscape layers, and distance-field shadows. We also worry about streaming hitches when crossing sub-level bounds. I want a pragmatic multi-day style execution graph that a lead could hand to the team: asset ingestion and naming, materials and VT validation on a representative mesh, LOD and Nanite/HLODs decision points, lighting and shadow interaction on hero props, profiling passes before and after batch edits, and a final sign-off gate that blocks polish until baseline FPS and streaming are acceptable. Make the ordering reflect real Unreal workflow risk—things that are expensive to redo should be gated earlier, and parallelizable work should be identifiable even if your tool emits a serial DAG.
